sections.title: Maintaining Pelvic Health Over Time (And What To Do If You Are Considering A Physiotherapist)

nodes.data: Understanding the Importance of Pelvic Health

Maintaining the health of your pelvic floor is crucial for various bodily functions, including bladder and bowel control, as well as sexual function. The pelvic floor muscles act like a supportive hammock across the base of your pelvis, holding in place the uterus, bladder, and bowel. Over time, these muscles can weaken due to factors such as pregnancy, childbirth, ageing, and high-impact sports. This weakening can lead to discomfort and conditions such as incontinence or pelvic organ prolapse.
A regular routine of
pelvic floor exercises

can significantly enhance pelvic health by strengthening these muscles. It's not only about doing the exercises but also about doing them correctly. Misguided efforts without proper technique can be ineffective or even harmful.
Key Exercises for Pelvic Floor Strengthening

The cornerstone of pelvic health is regularly performing pelvic floor exercises. These exercises, often referred to as Kegels, involve repeatedly contracting and relaxing the muscles that form part of the pelvic floor. Detailed guidance on how to correctly perform these exercises is crucial and can be found on resources like
female pelvic floor exercises
provided by Cambridge. These exercises are not only preventative but also therapeutic, offering benefits even to those already experiencing symptoms.

Moreover, integrating these exercises into your daily routine doesn’t require special equipment or a gym membership. They can be done discreetly and at almost any time – whether you’re at home, at work, or even while commuting. Consistency is key, as the benefits are best seen over time.
When to Consider Seeing a Physiotherapist

While preventive measures and home exercises are beneficial, certain situations require professional intervention. If you experience symptoms such as ongoing pelvic pain, difficulties with bladder and bowel movements, or a noticeable prolapse, it might be time to consult a physiotherapist. These professionals specialise in pelvic floor rehabilitation and can tailor a treatment plan that suits your specific needs.

Physiotherapists use various techniques, including manual therapy, education, and personalised exercise programs to improve pelvic floor function. They can also help in managing and alleviating the symptoms of related conditions, as detailed in
pelvic floor health guidelines
by the RCOG.

In conclusion, maintaining pelvic health is an ongoing process that benefits from both preventive exercises and professional advice when necessary. Early intervention by a physiotherapist can prevent the escalation of symptoms and improve quality of life. If you're considering this step, remember that it's not just about alleviating current symptoms but also about investing in your long-term health and wellness.

faqs.question: Why is it important for both men and women to maintain strong pelvic floor muscles?
faqs.answer: Strong pelvic floor muscles are crucial for both men and women because they support various pelvic organs, including the bladder, intestines, and in women, the uterus. These muscles contribute to urinary and bowel control, sexual function, and overall pelvic stability. Strengthening these muscles can prevent issues like incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse, and improve core strength and stability.

faqs.question: How do pelvic floor exercises, such as Kegels, improve sexual function?
faqs.answer: Pelvic floor exercises like Kegels improve sexual function by enhancing the muscle tone and blood circulation in the pelvic region. This increased muscle tone and circulation can lead to increased sensitivity and pleasure during sexual activity. For men, stronger pelvic muscles can contribute to erectile function and ejaculation control. For women, these exercises can increase arousal and help in achieving orgasm.

faqs.question: What are some common mistakes people make when performing pelvic floor exercises and how can they be avoided?
faqs.answer: One common mistake is using the wrong muscles during exercises, such as engaging the abdominal or thigh muscles instead of the pelvic floor. To avoid this, focus on tightening only the muscles that would stop urinary flow. Another mistake is holding one's breath, which can put additional pressure on the pelvic floor. It's important to breathe normally during the exercises. Lastly, overexerting or not practicing regularly can also be counterproductive. It is advisable to follow a consistent and well-structured exercise plan.

faqs.question: During pregnancy, how can pelvic floor exercises benefit expectant mothers, and are there any precautions they should take?
faqs.answer: During pregnancy, pelvic floor exercises can help manage the increased strain on the pelvic region due to the growing uterus. These exercises aid in maintaining bladder control, reducing the risk of prolapse, and potentially easing the childbirth process. Expectant mothers should focus on gentle exercises and avoid overexertion. It is recommended to consult a healthcare professional or a physiotherapist specialized in women's health to get personalized guidance and ensure that the exercises are done safely.

faqs.question: When should someone consider seeing a physiotherapist for pelvic health issues, and what can they expect during treatment?
faqs.answer: Individuals should consider seeing a physiotherapist for pelvic health issues if they experience symptoms like persistent pelvic pain, incontinence, discomfort during sexual activity, or signs of pelvic organ prolapse. During treatment, a physiotherapist will typically conduct a thorough assessment of pelvic floor function and tailor exercises and treatments to address specific issues. Treatments may include manual therapy, biofeedback, and personalized exercise programs designed to strengthen the pelvic floor and alleviate symptoms.

checklist: Step 1: Educate yourself on the anatomy and function of the pelvic floor muscles, understanding their role in supporting the bladder, intestines, and uterus (for women).
Step 2: Begin incorporating pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) into your daily routine, aiming for at least three sets of 10-15 contractions per day.
Step 3: If you are pregnant, consult with a healthcare professional about modifying pelvic floor exercises to accommodate changes in your body and ensure safety during pregnancy.
Step 4: Focus on proper technique for pelvic floor exercises, ensuring that you are lifting and squeezing the muscles correctly without tightening the abdomen, buttocks, or thighs.
Step 5: Integrate feedback from your body and adjust the intensity of the exercises based on comfort and any physical responses observed.
Step 6: Avoid common mistakes in pelvic floor exercises, such as holding your breath or pushing down instead of lifting up, by periodically re-evaluating your technique.
Step 7: Schedule regular check-ins with a healthcare professional or physiotherapist specialized in pelvic health to assess progress and receive personalized guidance.
Step 8: Gradually increase the difficulty and variation of pelvic floor exercises as you gain strength, incorporating longer holds and more complex movements as advised by your physiotherapist.
Step 9: Maintain a consistent routine, understanding that pelvic health is an ongoing commitment and requires regular attention to prevent issues.
Step 10: If experiencing any discomfort, pain, or lack of progress, consult with a healthcare professional to explore further diagnostic measures or alternative treatment options.

He clearly explained my hand injury and the proposed treatment approach. I left feeling confident and well supported. A great initial experience, and I would totally recommend him to anyone seeking expert physiotherapy care.

After a long time away from playing football, I decided to get back into and had some frustrating injuries getting in the way. After my first assessment from Jordan, he identified my underlying issues and set out a recovery and rehab programme. It didn’t take long to start seeing the benefits. With regular sessions we dealt with any new issues but continued work on the root cause. After great mix of hands on physio and pitch sessions I am now feeling in great condition. The nutritional advice along with pre and post game conditioning routines given, I feel will help me continue with the great progress and prolong my time in football. Been a top class experience with Jordan from start till now and the physical benefits and knowledge I am taking away is priceless.
I have been struggling with knee pain for 2 years and after a block of treatment with Jordan my pain is all but gone. Would absolutely recommend him to anyone.
